title: NOC 21200 – Architects | GTR Immigration
description: Architects play a central role in shaping cities, infrastructure, housing, and commercial developments across Canada. Classified under NOC 21200 (TEER 1), this occupation qualifies for multiple skilled-immigration pathways including Express Entry–aligned programs, Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs), and employer-supported work permits.

classification.category: 2 – Natural and applied sciences and related occupations
classification.teer: 1 – Occupations usually require a university degree
classification.major_group: 212 – Professional occupations in applied sciences (except engineering)
classification.minor_group: 2120 – Architects, urban planners, and land surveyors

duties: Consult with clients on renovation or new-build concepts
Design structures and prepare development plans
Create sketches, models, and digital renderings
Participate in contract negotiations and tendering
Coordinate with engineers, planners, and contractors
employment_requirements: Bachelor’s degree from an accredited architecture school or RAIC-recognized program
Three-year internship under a licensed architect
job_outlook: Between 2022–2031, Canada is expected to see 5,400 new architect positions, with around 5,700 job seekers—suggesting a competitive but stable immigration environment, especially for internationally licensed professionals with Canadian equivalency and provincial registration.
additional_information: Demand for architects remains strong nationwide, supported by housing development, infrastructure investment, urban renewal projects, and sustainability-driven construction.
Architectural and design firmsReal-estate developersConstruction companiesGovernment agenciesEngineering consultanciesSelf-employed practices
Annual earnings commonly range between $46,200–$115,500 CAD, depending on province, specialization, licensing, and experience.
Why Choose Canada as an Architect?

Continuous construction and housing demandProvincial nomination options for licensed architectsStrong wage growth in major citiesPR-linked skilled-worker pathwaysUniversal healthcareFamily sponsorship programsGlobally recognized design sector
